US small businesses are pulling back on investment as if we are in a crisis:

In March, only 16% of small businesses said that they plan on investing in CapEx over the next 6 months, the lowest reading since November 2009.

This percentage has declined -12 points since November 2024.

By comparison, the pre-2008 Financial Crisis and the 2020 pandemic levels were 30-35%.

Furthermore, the proportion of firms investing in CapEx over the last 6 months has declined -9 points since January, and remains below the long-term historical average.

This comes as policy uncertainty and elevated energy costs are weighing on small businesses.

All while they cite taxes, quality of labor, and inflation as their most important problems.

Small businesses are struggling in the US.

BREAKING: The S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF, $RSP, has seen -$2.0 billion in outflows so far in April, on track for its largest monthly outflow on record.

This comes after $RSP posted +$6.0 billion in inflows in February, the largest swing in flows since the fund's inception.

By comparison, the previous record monthly outflow was -$1.5 billion set in mid-2025.

This also suggests narrowing market breadth as investors are rotating back into large-cap tech stocks.

Money is flowing back into the Mag 7.
🚨 Trump just announced an Iran deal is being signed today in Pakistan.

Professional investors are rushing back into equities:

US equity positioning jumped +0.55 points last week, the largest weekly increase in 8 years.

This metric covers institutional investors like fund managers, hedge funds, and algorithm-based trading strategies.

Since 2010, there have been only 3 comparable increases in positioning: 2014, 2015, and 2016.

As a result, consolidated equity positioning is up to +0.20 points, the highest since January, from -0.50 in early April.

Positioning is now at neutral levels, having been lower than current levels ~51% of the time since 2010.

Wall Street is chasing this rally.

🚨 About 40% of Americans report having under $500 in cash savings

DO NOT LOOK AT THIS IMAGE.

Your stocks are up. You’re overexposed.

Just enjoy it.

There is absolutely no reason to look at the fundamentals right now. https://t.co/fetg5PRGgy

🚨 WHY IS NOBODY TALKING ABOUT THIS?

What’s holding the market up is getting thinner every week.

Every number below is pulled straight from the Fed, the Treasury, or the NY Fed. Go verify it yourself.

1: The Treasury General Account is vacuuming cash out of the system.

TGA balance closed at $971.16 billion on April 16, 2026.

That’s up from $924.44 billion the day before, and up from $638.78 billion a year ago.

That’s a 52.03% year over year jump in the government’s cash pile at the Fed.

Every dollar sitting in the TGA is a dollar removed from the banking system.

2: Bank reserves just got hit with one of the biggest weekly drains in years.

Reserve balances on the Fed’s H.4.1 fell by $203.34 billion in a single week, down to $2.98 trillion as of April 15, 2026.

Year over year, reserves are down $300.66 billion.

Liquidity is not tightening, IT’S DRAINING.

3: The Overnight Reverse Repo Facility is effectively zero.

RRP print on April 17, 2026 was $0.137 billion.

At its peak in late 2022, this facility was holding $2.55 trillion.

That entire $2.5 trillion cushion is GONE. There’s no buffer left in the system the way there was two years ago.

4: QT already did its damage before it ended.

QT officially ended on December 1, 2025 after running for over three years.

In that window, the Fed pulled more than $2 trillion of liquidity out of the system.

The balance sheet sits around $6.65 trillion today, but the damage from three years of runoff is already baked in. The system never got that liquidity back.

5: Year-end stress already showed up once.

The Standing Repo Facility spiked to $75 billion on December 31, 2025 before unwinding to ZERO on January 5.

That is a live warning shot that the plumbing is tight, not a theoretical risk.

6: Treasury market depth is cracking.

Morgan Stanley flagged reduced Treasury liquidity on March 25, 2026, using BrokerTec data and pointing to war-driven volatility as the source of stress.

Barclays followed on March 31, warning the Treasury market has outgrown primary dealer balance sheets to the point where it will likely need official intervention to keep functioning.

Top desks are openly saying the biggest debt market on the planet is structurally fragile.

7: Equity concentration is at record extremes.

Goldman flagged in its 2026 outlook that US stock market concentration is at HISTORIC HIGHS.

A handful of names are carrying the entire index while the liquidity underneath keeps shrinking.

What this setup actually means.

TGA swelling to $971 billion.

Reserves draining $203 billion in one week.

RRP at $0.137 billion.

Dealer balance sheets overwhelmed.

Concentration at record highs.

Repo stress already showing up at year-end.

That is not a healthy market. That is a market one bad headline away from a real dislocation.

The data above is public. H.4.1 from April 16. TGA from the Treasury daily statement. RRPONTSYD from FRED. Morgan Stanley and Barclays notes from late March.

Think about what actually happens if institutions decide to sell at the same time. The buyers on the other side are not real money with conviction.

They are algorithms, passive funds that have no choice but to follow the index down, and leveraged players who get margin called the second things start moving against them.

There’s no deep pool of fundamental buyers sitting in cash waiting to absorb a real sell-off at these prices.

The first wave of selling hits air, prices gap, that triggers the next wave.

And suddenly what looked like an orderly market becomes something very different, VERY FAST.

I’ve been doing this for a long time. When I make my next move in the markets, members of The Assembly hear it first.

The door is closed right now. I keep it that way on purpose.

Subscribe to @InTheAssembly with notifications so you don’t miss when it reopens.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

If you don’t do it, you might regret it later.
